name: Khepri
description: Khepri, also spelled Khepera, is an ancient Egyptian deity associated with the scarab beetle (Scarabaeus sacer) and the rising sun. The name Khepri derives from the Egyptian word 'ḫpr', meaning 'to come into being' or 'to transform'. As such, Khepri embodies the concept of spontaneous creation, renewal, and resurrection. He is frequently depicted as a scarab beetle, or as a human with a scarab beetle head, pushing the sun disc (representing Ra, the sun god) across the sky from east to west. This act symbolizes the daily rebirth of the sun and the cyclical nature of time and existence. Khepri is not typically associated with a specific cult or temple, but rather represents a fundamental cosmological principle integrated into broader Egyptian religious beliefs. His role highlights the Egyptians' deep fascination with the cycles of nature and their belief in the possibility of transformation and eternal life. He is often associated with the morning sun, while Ra is associated with the midday sun and Atum with the evening sun.
subtitle: He Who Comes Into Being

keyMyths.title: Khepri and the Solar Journey
keyMyths.description: Khepri's primary myth involves his role in pushing the sun disc across the sky. Each morning, he emerges from the eastern horizon as a scarab beetle, representing the newly reborn sun. He then propels the sun disc, Ra, across the heavens. This arduous journey is fraught with challenges, including battles against the forces of chaos in the underworld each night. Khepri's strength and determination ensure the sun's daily resurrection, guaranteeing light and order for the world. This daily cycle reinforces the concept of continuous renewal central to Egyptian beliefs.
keyMyths.source: Assmann, Jan. *Death and Salvation in Ancient Egypt*.

keyMyths.title: Khepri as a Self-Created Being
keyMyths.description: Because of the scarab beetle's apparent ability to spontaneously emerge from dung, Khepri was seen as a self-created being. This idea ties into the broader Egyptian creation myths, where deities like Atum are also believed to have brought themselves into existence. Khepri's self-creation emphasizes the power of transformation and the potential for life to arise from seemingly nothing. This myth highlights the divine power inherent in the natural world and the interconnectedness of all things.
keyMyths.source: Pinch, Geraldine. *Egyptian Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Goddesses, and Traditions of Ancient Egypt*.

keyMyths.title: Khepri and the Afterlife
keyMyths.description: Khepri's association with rebirth made him an important figure in Egyptian funerary beliefs. Scarab amulets, often inscribed with spells from the Book of the Dead, were placed on mummies to ensure the deceased's resurrection and passage into the afterlife. These amulets acted as a reminder of Khepri's power to transform and renew, offering hope for eternal life beyond the mortal realm. The scarab's symbolism provided comfort and assurance to the deceased and their loved ones.
keyMyths.source: Taylor, John H. *Death and the Afterlife in Ancient Egypt*.
